html.notes_text: This is a mystery beer made from unidentified bags of specialty grains left lying around my Home Brew Shop, unlabeled bags pf hops from my shop, unknown hops my brother grew in Colorado, and unlabeled yeast. The only ingredients I know for sure are the Maris Otter base malt, and the Warrior hops. All other malts and amounts are simply my best guess judging by grain color and aroma, and looking at what was missing from the inventory to try and identify the hops and yeast.

0004 Cascade Wheat Pale Ale, an American Pale Ale homebrew beer recipe
html.notes_text: OG: 1.048 / 12 degrees Plato IBU: 33 ABV: 5.1% Saturday, May 11, 2013 Brew Day Mash and Sparge .5 Campden Tab in 9 gal tap water Mash 168 F strike temperature, 3.75 gal, 12 lbs grain. Too hot, adjusted down with cold tap water Sparge 170 F Collect 6.5 gal, ran out of sparge water and had to heat more Next time: start with more water and have treated cold water available to prevent added chlorine Boil Pre-boil gravity: 1.040 15 min chiller into wort 5 min .5 Whirlfloc tab Chilled to 60 degrees in 12 min Dumped total contents of kettle into 6.5 gal glass carboy via ball valve. Tried filtering and was too difficult to keep up with kitchen strainer. decided prolonged exposure of beer to potential contaminants was not worth filtering break material and hops. Next time will try kettle valve screen. Aerated Wort, pitched yeast onto surface, allowed to hydrate 30 minutes with stopper in place. Aerated again for a few minutes and placed into closet at 65 degrees Sample 5/11 OG: 1.048 at 60 F. Very clear dark golden color. Looks good, tastes good. Took pictures. Tuesday May 21, 2013 (Day 10) Condition / Dry Hop dark gold and fairly clear, appears to be done with fermentation. kreusen has fallen and surface of liquid mostly clear, large sediment at bottom. Sample 5/21 FG: 1.010 at 60 F Tastes very good, no off flavors and good balance Racked to 5 gal glass carboy over 4 oz pellet Cascade Large sediment at bottom remained undisturbed and transferred very clear. Sediment at bottom and draining all kettle contents to primary does not appear to affect quality for 10 day fermentation upcoming targets: Tuesday 5/28 (Day 17) evening - sample, begin cold crash Friday 5/31 (Day 20) evening - transfer to keg and begin force carbonation with stone Friday 6/7 (Day 27) - cold condition complete and CO2 volume stabilized

#001 German pilsner
001-Hibiscus Tripel
html.notes_text: 3/3- Just brewed today, added a blowoff tube as to prevent the near inevitable over-fermentation. Chose to add the candi sugar a week in as to allow the yeast to eat up the more complex sugars first. Will update in a week when I add it in unless something of note happens. Used a starter with 2.25L of water and 225g of DME, stirred intermittently and an additional pack of yeast to obtain the near 300 billion needed for a fermentation this size. 3/9-At night, I added the Candi Sugar, the fermentation temp was about 65deg, and while I was at it, I checked the gravity (1.054). The taste was rather hoppy with no discernible hibiscus flavors. (All these observations were before the sugar was added.) Racked to secondary 3/24. Bottled on 6/30, 98 days later. Carbed with 100g of dextrose.

html.title: #001 Pacific Ale (extract), an American Amber Ale homebrew beer recipe
html.notes_text: Stone & Wood Clone. "Light on the palate, strong on the passionfruit and tropical flavours, this beer is by far our most popular offering. If you’re yet to try it, you won’t be disappointed. It’s an easy, sessionable beer, and it embodies everything we love about the Northern Rivers. Brewed using Australian Barley and wheat, it champions the striking and identifiable characteristics of the Galaxy hops. Pacific Ale is cloudy and golden with a big fruity aroma and a refreshing finish."

html.title: 001 - Puddingstone Pale Ale, an American Pale Ale homebrew beer recipe
html.notes_text: Batch #001 - Measured original gravity was only of the 3 gallon wort and was 1.085. Does not include the 2 gallons of spring water mixed so this is an inaccurate and irrelevant reading. Steeped Crystal Malt at 170 deg F for 30 min, when time had passed temp was 155 deg F. Pitched yeast at approx. 80 deg F. Sprinkled dry yeast on top. After 2 hours no activity. After 11 hours consistent bubbles about every 3 seconds. Bubbles continued until 92 hours after pitching yeast. Let sit in primary for 10 days before bottling. Priming mix is 1 cup water with 5 oz priming sugar. Bottled on 7/9/12. Label available for download at: http://www.lonjandis.com/beer/001puddingstonepaleale.png

html.title: (001) Punkin Blood
html.notes_text: Original Recipe: Pumpkin Ale Recipe Perfect for the season, this is a favorite beer of mine to brew this time of year (although I haven’t brewed in a long time), and it’s always good. This is mostly an extract-based recipe, though there is a short mash/rest with the pumpkin and malt. Use this recipe as-is, or as a basis for experimentation. Yield: 5 gallons of beer with an (approximate) alcohol by volume of 6 to 8% (depending on mash efficiency, use of brown sugar/molasses, amount of pumpkin, etc.). Ingredients: Fermentables: 6-10 pounds of pumpkin, roasted 1 pound of Vienna malt, 4L ½ pound crystal malt, 40L ½ pound malted wheat 6 pounds light or amber malt extract 1 cup brown sugar (optional) ½ cup molasses (optional) Hops and Spices: 1 ounce Mt. Hood hops (boiling) ½ ounce Hallertauer hops (finishing) ½ teaspoon vanilla ½ to 1 teaspoon pumpkin pie spices (see below) Yeast: Wyeast 1056, American Ale, or 1272, American Ale II Directions: The first thing to say is do not use canned pumpkin! Real pumpkin is the only way to go here, otherwise you’ll be dealing with a huge mess. You’ve been warned. You’ll first need to roast the pumpkin in the oven, similar to cooking squash. This softens the pumpkin and begins breaking it down. Cut the pumpkin into manageable pieces (should be cleaned, of course—old jack-o’lanterns work great), place in a shallow baking pan and add a bit of water to the pan. Roast in a 325°-ish oven for about an hour, or until soft. Or check a cookbook . There’s two ways you can incorporate the finished pumpkin: a partial mash-style method or simply a soak with the grains as the water heats. For the soak method, simply add the pumpkin and the grains to your pot of water, put it on the heat to boil. When it boils, remove the pumpkin and grains. For the partial mash, add the pumpkin and grains to hot water (ideally you want this mash to settle at 150 to 155 degrees Fahrenheit) and let rest for an hour. Sparge the pumpkiny wort from the mash, and add to your brew kettle. (Note: if you don’t have sparging equipment, so this removal is simply via a wire strainer.) Add the malt extract (6 pounds is for dry, you could easily get away with 7 pounds if it’s syrup), optional brown sugar and molasses, Mt. Hood hops and boil for 1 hour. After 45 minutes, add the finishing Hallertauer hops. At the very end of the boil, add the vanilla and pumpkin pie spices—these are volatile and adding them to the boil any sooner will essentially nullify their flavor and aroma contributions. Ferment for 1 week, or until primary fermentation settles down, and rack to a secondary for 2 more weeks. The secondary fermentation is not strictly necessary, but I like it for clarification and for letting the flavors mellow. You might also add the spices at this stage for a more pronounced presence. Bottle, priming with ¾ cup of corn sugar, and drink after a couple of weeks. Spices: I like a typical mixture of what you’d put into a pumpkin pie: cinnamon, nutmeg, allspice, mace, maybe ginger. I’d avoid buying the generic “Pumpkin Pie Spice” from the store, and mix your own instead—you’ll have more control over the outcome. This spice essence is one of the first things you notice when you open a bottle—yum! I’ve heard of recipes that don’t actually use pumpkin—seems weird to me. I personally think real pumpkin adds a unique flavor and texture to the finished beer that you just can’t replicate otherwise. And, a quick note about the hops. Mt. Hood is a nice spicy, slightly sweet hop (a variant of Hallertauer, actually) that seems to complement this beer well. For finishing, you don’t have to stick to Hallertauer; play with it a bit. Cascade might be a nice finish, or if you want to reduce the hop bite, omit the finishing hop altogether.

0011 Citra Double IPA Clone, an Imperial IPA homebrew beer recipe
html.notes_text: Yeast started in 2 L Water : 200 g Briess Pilsen Light DME at 8pm May 17 2012. Refrigerated at 2am May 19 2012. Steeped grains for 20 minutes in 3.5 gallons of 155°F water. Bring water to boil for 60 minutes at 1:55pm and added 60 minute hops and 3 pounds DME. 5At 2:10pm added 45 minute hops. At 2:20pm added 1 pound 8 ounces DME. At 2:30pm added 3 pounds DME and 10 ounces corn sugar. Paused timer for 5 minutes to return to boil. At 2:45pm added 15 minute hops and Irish moss. At 2:55pm added 5 minute hops. At 2:59pm added 1 minute hops. Ended boil at 3pm. Transferred to 2° 2012/05/28. Dry hops added 2012/06/10. Bottled 2012/06/17 with 4 oz priming corn sugar.

html.title: (002) Almond IPA, an American IPA homebrew beer recipe
html.notes_text: The grain bill included some left over grains from a pumpkin ale. Although this turned out great, I would probably modify the grain bill to lighten the color if I were to brew it again.

html.title: #002 American Pale Ale, an American Pale Ale homebrew beer recipe
002 - Better Be Chocolate Malts
html.notes_text: *chocolate malt was not scientifically measured Mash 11:35am grain added to 2 gallons pre-boiled water at 160F add tap water ice to bring to 158F 12:40pm sparge at 158F after grain is added 1pm remove grain Wort Boil Worts combined, DME added, heat on. Boil begins, but is not rolling. Rolling boil established at 43min. Crystal hops added at 30min. Amarillo hops added at 15min. Cool / Pitch Cool for .5 hour changing water bath, last time added ice. Transfer to fermenter and aerate. Wort was too hot, probably in the 80sF (hot-side aeration?). Top off with 1+ gallons pre-boiled water to 5 gallon total. Forgot to remove yeast from fridge, so RT water was used to warm. Shaken well, and pitched. OG: 1.040 @80F 1.045 @RT (~68F) Fermentation Try to keep at 70+F for first day. Fermentation bubbles through airlock at 27 hours and 73F.

html.title: #002 Brown Bitter, an American IPA homebrew beer recipe
html.notes_text: Brewed 11/24/2012 BIAB w/sparge & squeeze. Fermented at 65F for 3 weeks, overly bitter with lack of aroma, decided to dry hop with Saaz for ~10 days. Bottled 12/20/2012, still bitter, but the dry hop has taken some of the edge off. Will have to let this one bottle condition for a while.

html.title: #002 - Caribou Slobber Brown Ale, an American Brown Ale homebrew beer recipe
002-Chocolate Cayenne Dry Stout
html.notes_text: Brewed 3/24, pitched activator pack without a starter. Steeped grains at 155ish for 30m in 1.25gal, followed by 5m of "tea bag" sparging in a gal at 170. Added the DME in 4lb at beg, 1.5 at 15m. Will allow to ferment for about two weeks in primary 3/31- Gravity at 1.021. 4/6- Transferred to Secondary, Racked on top of 4 small cut up cayenne and the chocolate extract. Gravity was 1.013, and tastes FANTASTIC. 4/22- Added 4 more peppers, not much flavor yet. 4/25- Good HOT bite. Bottled with 4 oz of Lactose to round out flavor and 2.3oz of Corn Sugar. Will Condition for 4 weeks at around 64. 5/23-

html.notes_text: Bottling time gravity (after 3 days on with the cucumber) 1.004 - this has raised the ABV to 7.7%... I'm guessing the yeast ate through some of the sugars in the cucumber?

#002 Little Creatures Pale Ale Clone (extract), an American Pale Ale homebrew beer recipe
html.notes_text: From the Little Creatures website: The beer that inspired this crazy journey, ours is a hop driven Pale Ale bursting with flavour and aroma. Full-bodied yet refreshing, the bitter finish will leave your tastebuds craving more. Malts: Pale malt, Munich, Caramalt, & Wheat malt Hops: EKG (UK) kettle bittering, Cascade (US/Aus/NZ) and one other hop variety (based on seasonal variations in the best hops available) in the whirlpool and Cascade (US/Aus), Chinook (US) and one other hop variety in the hopback. Flavours: Burnt toffee / citrus / stonefruits ABV: 5.2% | IBU: 36 | EBC: 20

html.title: 002 - London 2012 Olympic ESB, an Extra Special/Strong Bitter (English Pale Ale) homebrew beer recipe
html.notes_text: Used 3gal of water from the spring on my street, 7.8pH level. For steeping, brought 3 gal to 170F and steeped for 30 min, end temp was ~160F. Used muslin bags for hop additions and removed at end of boil. Used stainless steel wort chiller to cool wort to 78F, added 2 gallons cold bottled spring water to bring down to 68F. Pitched yeast at 68F and stored in primary for 9 days at 72F. Visual fermentation started around 16hrs and ended around 96hrs. Bottled on 8/7/12 into 41 12oz bottles and 1 2L growler. Primed with 5oz sugar and 2oz tap water. Label available for download at: http://www.lonjandis.com/beer/002olympicesb.png

html.title: 003 - Allagash White Duplicator
html.notes_text: Started wort with 3.5gal of water from the spring on my street, 7.8pH level. Steeped grains in water for 30min starting at 170F. Hop additions and spiced were added in muslin bags and pulled out at flameout. Used stainless steel wort chiller to cool to about 80F and added 2gal cold spring water to bring to about 70F. Poured wort into primary through strainer. Pitched yeast at 70F. Visual fermentation began within 4hrs. Within 24hrs, krausen was spitting out the top of airlock, which was replaced with blowoff tube into empty spring water gallon jug. After 4 days, moved to secondary with blowoff and fermentation continued for over 24hrs. Sat in secondary for 9 days and bottled on 9/3/12 into 26 12oz bottles and 12 24oz bottles. During bottling, put sanitizer into dishwasher and had major issues with foam. DO NOT PUT SANITIZER INTO DISHWASHER Tried using vinegar & rubbing alcohol to kill suds but didn't work. Worried about vinegar/alcohol flavor so soaked bottles in sanitizing solution and dried before bottling. During siphoning, began with tube too large and wouldn't get a proper siphon going. Ended up siphoning through tube that still had krausen residue in it even after washing & sanitizing. Conditioning in bottles at 72F for 14 days, then 42F for 7 days before consuming. Bottle label available for download at: http://www.lonjandis.com/beer/003allagashduplicator.png

html.title: 003 - Roommate Points IPA, an American IPA homebrew beer recipe
html.notes_text: *~1.5L 1056 starter (1.040 OG) three days in advance *Short boil-over about 10min into boil, relatively contained *Measured gravity immediately after racking to primary: 1.072 @78F (temperature adjusted to 1.074) *Moved to fermentation storage (ambient 64F) *Pitched 4hrs after brewing (72F) *Visible bubbling through airlock 3hrs from pitch *Strong fermentation, noticed krausen through airlock 36hrs after pitch...cleaned and replaced *bottling with 3.8oz corn sugar
